desc:
Following the shores of the Anduin to the south, these fields get cornered in by
the border of the Gladden Fields and the steep banks of the Great River. The
earth is damp and moist, but the grey moss of the west has not completely filled
the plains. The rain that falls gets transported either into the swampy grounds
in the west, or the river in the east. This leaves the ground with a healthy
moisture giving place for flowers and fragile stems of grass. To the west a
sharp slippery decline leads into the marsh.
name: Border of the Gladden Fields

desc:
Dead branches and leaves cover the soft moss on the wet ground. In the north,
the origin of these can be spotted in the form of a small forest reaching into
the marshes. To the east a sharp ascent up to the higher fields above lies in
the way of travelling. It is covered with sturdy straws of grey grass spreading
out even into the plains above. To the south a heavy thicket of ensnared
branches and rotting grass covers the shores of a gathering of water.
name: Descent into the Gladden Fields

desc:
The undulating marshland reaches out, covering the ancient lake floor with moss
and lichen that peaks up between the countless pools of dark water. An
occasional shrub or tree draws its silhouette against the sky beyond. Roots and
undulating water-soaked lichen make the way west impossible to pass.
name: Misty Peaks and Sinks

desc:
Pools of water running across the land make the rather large patches of moss
look like islands in a sea. Looking closer, the water is however rather shallow,
with more moss and mud beneath. Only the mire knows how deep this moss runs. Far
in the south, beyond mile upon mile of marshland, the two rivers Anduin and
Gladden River connect in a sparkling display. A sharp drop filled with flowing
water cuts the undulating marshes in the west.
name: Drenched Lowlands

desc:
This little piece of the marsh neighbours the bends of the two, nearby rivers.
The Gladden makes a turn towards the south right to the west, while the Anduin
follows its considerably straight route in the south-east. The soil is humid,
and its lowest parts are often transformed into puddles.
name: Marshes between the Gladden and the Anduin

desc:
The large Anduin unevenly borders this quite flat marsh to the south-east. The
humid soil has been covered by some high weeds, but there are a couple of tall
bushes that are noticed here and there. Several puddles, all hidden under the
vegetation, may sometimes be a cold surprise.
name: A Humid Riverbank

desc:
The water from the gently flowing Anduin trickles in from the east. A soft,
almost distant, clucking noise of the river can be heard, as if coming from
further away than the actual current. The ground is soggy, filled with water and
moisture. The water has seemingly coagulated in the ground and formed a thicker
form of liquid, greyish in colour. An itching mist rises from the soft earth,
leaving little sight of the ground. To the north a large cluster of rotting
branches and grass has gathered near a pool of water. To the sides sharp slopes
makes travelling difficult, dropping into the marshes in the west and the rapid
water of the Anduin in the east.
name: Entering the Gladden Fields

desc:
This thin strip of the marshes is stuck between the parallel riverbeds of the
Gladden to the west and of the Anduin to the east. Towards the south, part of
the vegetation-covered land dives slowly below the water level, allowing both of
the rivers to meet each other.
name: North of the Meeting of the Rivers

desc:
In the surrounding areas, with the Anduin in the west and the Gladden stretching
beyond it, are plains upon plains. This patch of hills stands out as a break in
the lands. To the east the hills stretch a bit further, but quickly flatten out
again. In the south a small forest grows on the feet of the hills. The ground is
still covered with thick grass, but on some of the slopes rocks and gravel have
been left bare under the sky.
name: Flowing Hills

desc:
The hills in the north quickly flatten out here making room for trees to gather
and form a colony next to the Anduin. The ground is soft and mossy, watered by
the Anduin and numerous stains of water, gathered in pools beneath the trees.
The trees themselves are covered in moss and lichen, and many have started to
decay, rotting away in the wet climate.
name: Mossy Forest

desc:
With the ground being raised slightly higher than the northern forest, this area
is rather safe from the Anduin, which flows to the north-west. The bottoms of
the tree trunks are covered by some green moss and some rotten branches can be
seen among the fallen leaves.
name: A Slightly Wet Forest

desc:
The ground, bearing patches of fallen leaves, is rather flat here, except in the
south where it transforms into low slopes. The forest, mix of beeches and oaks,
continues in all the directions, although only the northern part remains
somewhat dense.
name: A Mixed Forest

desc:
The earth of these tree-covered slopes seems to be mixed with quite some large
and irregular pebbles. To the south, the relief increases considerably, where
most of the trees have faded and are finally leaving the place to a tall, rather
bare hill. However, the hill can barely be noticed, through the thinner parts of
the surrounding forest.
name: Woody Slopes

desc:
The Anduin River borders to the west of this rather thin and mixed forest, which
then thickens to the east. There is some relief, slight mounds and depressions
here and there, however insignificant compared to the southern small and wooded
hills.
name: A Forest Bordering the Anduin

desc:
Small, thorny thickets are growing here and there, raising the relief of this
otherwise rather flat riverbank, save from its south-eastern part maybe. It is
bordered to the northwest by the Anduin, where some relatively small trees can
also be found, especially near the eastern forest.
name: A Woody Riverbank

desc:
Groups of young oaks and rowans stand out on these windswept hilltops. More
trees can be seen in the south, where dense bushes border the outskirts of a
forest. Bare outcrops of rock and fields of small flowers fill the hollows
between the hills, while in the east a steep slope leads down to a patch of
thick vegetation and grassy fields.
name: Sparsely Wooded Hills

desc:
The slim trunks of the young trees that grow here bend slightly whenever there
is a gust of wind. Brown leaves, fallen from the rowans and birches during last
autumn, still cling to the twigs that stick out among the mossy rocks and
stumps. The ground is damp, and the soil is bare near a spot where a large
animal has been digging for roots or mushrooms to eat.
name: Outskirts of a Forest

desc:
These small hills, some only being low mounds, slightly rise up southwards. As
they do, they appear to slowly lose their trees, finally matching the bare and
low eminences that are visible in this direction. Westwards, they slowly fade
into a rather bare and flat plain.
name: Forested Hills

desc:
While there are wild weeds and grasses in growth upon the front of the southern
hill, no thorn can be distinguished among them. The hill itself appears to be
part of a larger portion of hills, ranging to the south. The nearest slope is
nearly vertical and completely covered by climbing weeds.
name: Grassland in Front of a Hill

desc:
Taller hills overlook these lower ones from the west and east. All the nearby
slopes are pretty steep, each covered by a slippery moss, making climbing the
slopes to be nearly impossible. Besides reaching the forested hills that are to
the north, it appears the terrain is safe to move southwards, along the flat
parts between the slopes.
name: Low Hills

desc:
The western slope of this tall hill, rather tight and slippery, leads to a sea
of steep mounds, that is much darker than their surroundings. Though nearly
vertical, the northern incline appears possible to climb down using the large
and numerous stones that have been partly embedded in the earth.
name: A Tall Hill

desc:
Unlike the similar mounds that are visible westwards, to the east stands a tall,
green hill, having a top that is seemingly strewn with small bushes. While the
slippery vegetable matter covering all of the area nearly prevents anyone from
climbing any of the slopes, a safe and flat path can be found towards the south
and the north.
name: Among the Hills

desc:
This tall hill is easily reachable from the northern one, by following a common,
curved crest. While the western slope is rather steep and slippery, leading to a
lower and natural eminence, the southern landscape is made of plains. They
appear to be reachable by a bush-covered incline.
name: A Tall Hill

desc:
The earth in this area is very slick and the thin, wet moss that has grown on
the surrounding slopes does not make their climb less dangerous. The flat path
between the hills quickly stops east after a few of dead-ends, while the
southern one is blocked by a long mound.
name: Among the Hills

desc:
The hills here are rather small, but their slopes are steep and slippery. It is
possible to safely walk and ride between them to the east and south, judging by
its appearance. Besides a few dead-ends, there is no safe passage visible that
leads northwards towards the higher hills. The tops of the trees of a forest are
noticed behind the western hills.
name: Among the Hills

desc:
The slopes of these small hills are slippery and very tight, causing them to be
very difficult to climb. The bottom of the slopes between the hills, while
strewn with sharp protruding rocks, appears to be rather safe. A somewhat usable
path can be found towards the north and the south.
name: Among the Hills

desc:
The hills here are much smaller than the ones that can be seen northwards in the
distance. Their slopes are less steep, especially the one which leads to a lone,
large mound to the south. The trees appear to border this range of hills in both
directions to the west and east.
name: Small Hills

desc:
Unlike the northern hills, this tall one is reachable using easy slopes. While
the eastern slope soon transforms into a slightly wooded area, a similar mound
can be seen westwards. However, the western eminence is lacking the several
shrubs that are growing atop of this one. Distinguished among these are many
boulders, partly buried into the ground.
name: Hill Overlooking a Trail

desc:
A couple of small trees is in growth on the top of this hill, separated from the
northern forest by a bare and steep slope. While the hill continues east,
towards the west and south there are some long and curved slopes that appear to
finally lead to what seems to have been a trail long ago.
name: Hill Overlooking a Trail

desc:
The beech-covered, wide mounds that slightly hinder the movement in this area
are quite flat, compared to the tall hill that stands westwards and is visible
through the thinner parts of the surrounding foliage of the trees. Although the
bottom of some of the depressions is wet, no moss that can be seen grows on the
base of the trunks.
name: Woody Hills

desc:
This appears to be the southwestern part of this thin forest. The trees in
growth here are mainly beeches, but they mix with oaks towards the north. The
remains of an old trail can be seen loosely bordering it, with a couple of lone,
smaller trees growing among its dislocated slabs.
name: A Thin Forest

desc:
This thin, oaken forest is replaced in the west by a rather bare, green hill. To
the north stand mildly high bushes, however spaced apart. In all the other
directions, the forest continues, even though its tree species vary. There are
more oak trees eastwards, but beeches are in growth to the south.
name: Thin Forest near the Anduin

desc:
The trees here are less sparse than the surrounding pieces of the thin forest.
The forest contains mainly oaks, except to the south, where they are slowly
replaced by beech trees. The growth of these trees comes to an end quite fast on
the unstable, eastern hill slope. There are large holes that were left by the
fallen trees, revealing some stone blocks that lie within.
name: The Centre of a Thin Forest

desc:
While the trees are slightly less thick than those which stand northwards, they
are quite tall in height. This is probably due to all of the shadows cast down
by the steep slopes of the hills, which are located to the east and south. At
earth level, this area is very gloomy and rather damp, while the soil is covered
by some sparse dark grass.
name: The Border of a Thin Forest

desc:
This piece of the thin forest is constituted of sparse, but rather tall oaks.
The size of these trees begins to decrease to the west, leaving the area to be a
bush-covered plain. Towards the east, the top of a seemingly very old wall seems
to emerge from the slope of the hill, its use probably forgotten a long time
ago.
name: A Thin Forest

desc:
This part of the thin forest, bordered by the Anduin to the west and some bare
hills to the north and the east, is growing on some mounds itself. The nearby
slopes contain some piled, large rocks, which are mostly buried in the earth and
vegetable matter.
name: A Raised Thin Forest

desc:
For some reason, maybe from the unstable ground, the forest that circles around
this bushy marsh to the east and the south, never expanded itself here, leaving
place for wild weeds and mild bushes to grow on. The Anduin borders the place in
the remaining directions.
name: Between the Anduin and a Forest

desc:
While it is possible to follow the crests to reach the northern hills, among
which a taller one stands out, the eastern and southern surroundings consist of
quite smaller hills. The small hills are separated from the rest by very tight
and mossy slopes. The western, woody slope leads to a thin forest.
name: Among the Hills

desc:
While the northern landscapes are rather flat, save from a small forest in the
north-east, it becomes a sea of hills south from here. The nearby hills to the
west and south from are continuous enough, that it is possible to reach them
without having to climb much. However, the hills in the east are pretty small
and only reachable by a tight, slippery slope.
name: On the Top of a Hill

desc:
The height of this hill allows a great view on a small lake that is noticed to
the immediate north-west, where the two rivers meet. On the other hand, there is
a thin forest that stops at mid-height of the southern slope that does not
permit anything to be watched in that direction. Although some bushes can be
seen near the last trees, the top of the hill is rather bare, with its grassy
monotony only broken by some large stones.
name: Hill Overlooking the Anduin

desc:
This old trail, which wild weeds have now settled upon, is following a vague,
west-east direction, from the Anduin towards the dark shape of Mirkwood. Then at
some point, it makes a minor detour to the north, where it meets a blurred form
among the high grasses, which could be the remnants of another trail.
name: An Ancient Trail

desc:
This old trail follows a west-east axis, but makes a gentle curve around the
slope of the northern hill. It does not seem to have been used frequently, for
large weeds are growing between most of the slabs. However, some of the plants
have been recently crushed, possibly by some lost traveller or large animal.
name: Ancient Trail

desc:
This old trail can be guessed to meet the Anduin, which is several miles to the
west, but it is hard to tell whether it ever reaches the shadows of Mirkwood to
the far east. Its slabs have been laid on the lower part of the gentle slope of
the northern hill. Some of the slabs have been broken by whatever has travelled
upon them.
name: Ancient Trail

desc:
This old trail appears to follow a vague west-east direction, however entire
parts of it have disappeared. The used slabs have either been looted or maybe
simply buried under the vegetable matter over the time. There are some small
trees, probably the result of the nearby, northern forest which appears to
further dislocate the path.
name: Ancient Trail by a Wood

desc:
The nearby mounds and depressions are easily noticeable, even through the forest
that is surrounding them. While most of the forest consists of beech trees, a
few weeds are strewn over the rough mix of earth and angular pebbles. While the
relief is less visible to the north, a whole range of steep hills stands to the
west.
name: Woody Hills

desc:
The hills here are rather low and would actually be better described as a
succession of mounds and depressions. The soil is grassy and mixed with some
irregular pebbles. There are also some lonely trees, probably the offspring of
the southern forest, which can be seen marking the landscape here and there.
name: Approach of the Hills

desc:
The accentuated relief of the plain here nearly hinders travel, however, it is
still easy to avoid the steepest slopes. While patches of mild bushes are in
growth among the flat parts, none of this vegetation has grown above the size of
an average shrub.
name: An Uneven Plain

desc:
While these plains are somewhat uneven, made so by small mounds of earth, their
slopes seem no taller than the short bushes that have been strewn over the rest
of the land. There are some rare pebbles that are visible through the thinner
parts of the grass layer which completely covers the soil.
name: Plains near a Small Forest

desc:
The bottoms of all the slight depressions here on this somewhat, uneven plain
are too muddy to contain any drinkable water. It is, however, well enough to
allow a luxuriant vegetation to grow, parasite weeds covering the grass, a
lonely boulder and the trunks of the few trees that grow here.
name: Plains near a Small Forest

desc:
The old slabs of this part of the ancient trail appear to have remained in a
somewhat decent shape, covered only by weeds and vegetable matter. The road,
bending to the south, is breaking its relatively straight course coming from the
west, but soon it looks to change its direction once again.
name: A Bend in an Ancient Trail

desc:
Mounds and terrain depressions strew this uneven plain, however, they are not
tall enough to hinder movement. Some high grasses of some sort are covering
everything, their monotony broken here and there by a dark bush or a partly
buried boulder. A range of steep hills border the area to the west.
name: An Uneven Plain

desc:
Growing near a range of hills in the west, this brushwood comprised of young
trees and bushes is slowly turning into a forest. Many of the oaks are already
taller than most Men, and slender birches have sprouted out among them during
the past few summers. Some older trees can be seen to the southwest, where a
forest continues towards the hilltops. Wide, grassy plains spread out away from
the hills as far as the eye can see.
name: A Small Coppice at the Hillside

desc:
Small dirt paths, covered in old animal tracks, lead through the plains towards
areas of vegetation in the north and in the west. High hay covers the ground
here, and the tops of bushes and saplings that grows here and there have been
chewed off by deer or other large animals. In some places, tiny tufts of
feathers or fur stick to the hardy straws near the ground, hinting at the fact
that a multitude of small animals live in these fields.
name: Animal Paths

desc:
A smooth carpet of grass covers these slopes and fields, creating an unbroken
surface that seems to continue very far in every direction. Even rocks and
bushes are scarce here, and those that can be found are far too small to provide
cover for anyone but the tiniest animals. A single animal path leads to the
northeast, however, and the thickening vegetation suggests that water might be
found in that direction.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
This pond of water, starts out not more than a foot or two deep here, gradually
turning into a deeper pool in the north. Thick bushes and stunted trees grow on
tussocks of grass, sinking their clumps of roots into the shallow water. While
moving around through the bushes, some empty nests among the roots can be
noticed, and branches sway in some places as if small birds were hopping on
them.
name: Bird Nests in the Bushes

desc:
Tall ferns along with round bushes with thorny branches grow in clusters in this
field of bracken. The shrubbery is most dense on the bottom of a moist
depression, which separates the sloping grounds in the southeast from the wide
plains extending to other directions. An old trunk, buried in evergreen moss,
can be seen lying among the thick ferns down there; dead leaves cover the rest
of the ground.
name: Among the Bushes and Bracken

desc:
This musty cave seems large and dry enough to keep supplies or even a small band
of travellers protected from the elements. The cave was clearly not formed
naturally; a layer of straws lies spread on the floor, and some of the roots
sticking out of the earth seem to have been chopped off with a shovel or some
other tool. A single gush of water trickles down the surface of a rock in the
back wall, where it harmlessly soaks into the ground.
name: A Storage Cave

desc:
Passing through dense growths of bushes and bracken, winding paths lead down
into this quiet hollow surrounded by brushwood and small hillocks. The slopes
that lead away from here are so steep, and the vegetation is so dense, that the
rays of the sun do not reach directly into the hollow. Everything is cloaked in
twilight; clusters of horsetails grow under the trees, covering the ground with
a feathery shroud of green and grey.
name: Horsetails in the Shade

desc:
A wide stretch of short grass leads straight to the south, through the plains.
Somehow this spot does not seem to have formed naturally; tall plants grow
everywhere in the surrounding fields except here, yet there are no signs of
cattle or anything that might rid this area of such vegetation. Perhaps the soil
is too dry or the bedrock too near for any bushes or trees to take root.
name: A Fairway through the Plains

desc:
No shelter from the wind can be found on these widespread plains of grass. In
some places the hay is lying low, flattened by the last rain, revealing rocks
and other small bumps on the ground that might have otherwise remained hidden
from sight. Keeping an eye on the features of the open sky seems to be the only
way to keep a direction while travelling through these vast plains.
name: Wide, Grassy Plains

desc:
A field of yellowed hay and tall perennial plants spreads out here. Some of the
plants have had their dry cores picked clean by local birds; others are still
spreading their seeds into the breeze. Sparse bushes grow in the north, while
the fields of hay continue to the west as well as towards the sloping grounds in
the eastern reaches of these plains.
name: High Hay

desc:
Sandy depressions break the smooth carpet of grass in many places on these
gently sloping fields. Small puddles of water have formed on the bottom of many
such hollows, and tiny tracks of birds can be noticed around them. Sparse bushes
surround the areas of sand, and there seems to be a thick shrubbery at the
bottom of the slope to the north.
name: A Gentle Slope

desc:
The ground begins to slope gently upwards in the east, as the even, bushy plains
extending to the northwest come to an end here. The slopes are covered in short,
hardy bushes, but climbing up and down seems easy enough even without grabbing
the vegetation. A small stream, not more than two feet wide, streams in from the
east, babbling cheerfully until it gradually dries up while running towards the
bottom of the slope.
name: Grassy Slopes

desc:
The smoothly curving slope suddenly turns into an unnaturally flat terrace.
There are no signs of any recent farming in the area, but the shape of the field
and the narrow furrows running through it in neat lines suggests that many years
ago something was grown here. Currently the ground is covered in tangled vines,
thistles and small woody-stemmed shrubs, and the field does not seem readily
arable with all the roots and moss smothering the soil.
name: An Overgrown Garden

desc:
A smooth carpet of grass covers the hilltops and small valleys between them.
Travelling among the hills seems easy, even if keeping one's direction might
require climbing on a hillock and taking a look around occasionally. Nothing
taller than flowers grows on the round, windswept hilltops, and if there is any
shelter from the weather, the thick bushes enshrouding the hillsides keep it
hidden well. In the east, even taller bushes dominate the rising landscape.
name: Smooth Hills

desc:
An even meadow of tiny flowers spreads out here above the slope going down in
the north. The field is surrounded by an almost perfect circle of trees, most of
them burly elms of indeterminable age. There are also stumps and fallen trunks
of dead trees; one of them is so thick that even the tallest of Men would have
trouble putting his arms around it. A small pool of water has formed on the
bottom of a depression on the western slope at the edge of the wood; there might
be a spring nearby.
name: A Circle of Trees

desc:
A short, curved tunnel, maybe five or six feet high, leads into this circular
cavern under the hill. The long roots that are hanging from the ceiling and
reaching for the stony floor seem to keep the soil together; a cave-in does not
seem likely, even though loose dirt is slowly piling up near the back wall. Some
lengths of wood stick out of the soil, implying that perhaps this cave was once
used as a storage or even a shelter.
name: A Hole under the Hill

desc:
Solitary, tall pines, standing above the field of moss and lichen here, do not
offer much shelter against the sun or the rain. The ground is sandy and dry, and
the only plants thriving here seem to be the heathers, which grow in thick
clusters covering the roots of the pines. Old stumps sticking out of the ground
here and there are grey, probably ant-infested, and seem to break apart easily.
name: A Dry Heath

desc:
Oblong bushes, many of them taller than Men, shade the slopes and hillsides with
their foliage. Some of the slopes are so steep that anyone would have
difficulties climbing on them, but it is not hard to find a twisting route
amidst the shrubbery to lead around the most difficult spots. Bird droppings
cover the ground under some of the cliff tops that are hardest to reach; perhaps
these are nesting grounds for eagles or hawks.
name: A Bushy Hillside

desc:
Far more akin to a track than to a road, this old trail takes a corner here,
bending to the north and the east. By the side of the track stand a few,
long-dead trees that apparently provide a resting spot for the local birdlife.
Other than those dead trees, only some tough, grey-green grasses and the
occasional juniper bush grow here.
name: Bend in the Trail

desc:
A few, old logs have been dragged here by someone not too long ago, creating a
makeshift camp. A heap of old coals that once were a camp fire is still lying
around. East of here, one can discern a trail or track of some sort. South of
here, a plain that stretches out for long while. However, the plain looks as if
it has been split into two different elevations; a higher eastern part and a
lower western part.
name: Plains

desc:
This place bears the marks of significant geological upheavals that took place
aeons ago. Some unthinkable force has somehow managed to crack the plain open,
and an escarpment has risen out along the fault line. Looking south, one can see
a reddish rock wall rising out of the plain along the fault. The wall appears to
continue for miles and miles.
name: A Fault

desc:
A couple of boulders form a strange formation here, as two seemingly separate
chunks of rock are piled upon each other. Their combined shapes somehow appear
to resemble the head of a bird of prey. Around it, a number of bushes and ferns
are growing. A bit further away from the boulders, a tough, grey-green grass
begins to grow. Looking further out from the rocks, one can see that this grass
is the staple type of vegetation here.
name: Plains by the Eagle Rock

desc:
An abrupt change of terrain vis-a-vis the high and thick forest west of here,
these plains are almost bereft of all types of vegetation higher than a few
feet. Only a few hardy, juniper bushes contrast to the uniformity of the rolling
plains that reach around east and south of here. West of here, a giant boulder
rests on the flat plain.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
Tough weeds, hardy bushes and withered rocks dominate this rugged plain. One
would be safe to assume that this is truly untouched wilderness. If there were
any settlers here once, they would soon have been driven away by the obvious
lack of water, fertile soil and security. At least the road that runs north from
here can provide the traveller with a route to more enjoyable destinations.
name: Rough Plain

desc:
Quite a sharp rise, this ridge-line overlooks the vast and forlorn plain west of
here. Far to the west, the Misty Mountains can barely be discerned as a haze at
the horizon. The vegetation here is quite sparse: only a few, tough grasses and
the occasional briar or shrub cling to the reddish earth that comprises this
field.
name: Beginning of a Ridge

desc:
Whereas the rock wall that looms east of here appears quite unclimbable, it
looks as though it declines both in height and steepness north of here.
Westwards, a rolling, grassy plain stretches out towards a faraway forest. At
this spot, however, only a sharp, tough, grey-green grass manages to thrive.
name: Plains by the Escarpment

desc:
Although it is slightly lower than its surroundings, this field is still exposed
to the winds that blow across the undulating plains. Gazing north, one can
discern the silhouette of some kind of rock formation. Southwards, a slight rise
in the grass-covered plain provides a higher vantage point.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
Weather-worn and rugged, these fields somehow manage to retain a lush quality,
mostly because of the luxuriant grasses that make up the main part of the
vegetation here. To the west, a mixed forest lines the horizon, whereas the
grassy plains roll out towards a faraway rock wall to the south-east.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
An old and gnarled, yet unmistakably still alive, iron oak is the only thing
higher than a man growing here on this plain. More than likely, the old tree
used to belong to the forest further west of here, but sometime long ago, the
rest of the trees vanished, leaving this sole old warrior of a tree to enjoy its
solitude through the ages.
name: Plains by a Forest

desc:
This diminutive hillock, a part of the plain that reaches around in all
directions, rises slightly higher than the lands themselves, thus providing a
view of the grasslands that extends in all directions save west, where a tall
forest grows a few miles away. Further east, the plains roll on towards
something that looks like a rock wall.
name: Small Rise

desc:
Looking south or west, one sees only a long and reddish cliff wall that rises
out of the plain along what must be a geological fault line. Vegetation-wise,
this field hosts a few, hardy grasses and bushes. Along the very edge of the
cliff wall, pockets of water must have gathered at times, since one still can
see old animal tracks among the moist and muddy patches of clay.
name: Plains by the Escarpment

desc:
Here, the soft, reddish rock face of the escarpment sustains quite a large patch
of ivy that covers a sizeable part of the wall. Some juniper bushes crouch at
the base of the rock face. In all directions save east, a rolling country
unfolds, here and there dotted with clumps of darkish forests.
name: Plains by the Escarpment

desc:
Here, in the heartland of these ever-rolling grasslands, a few small but tough
bushes have found a home in a small crevice-like depression. Far to the east, a
high cliff-wall rises from the plain, effectively separating these lower fields
from whatever may lie beyond the escarpment.
name: Undulating Fields

desc:
Sometime not too long ago, this area was scorched by some kind of fire that
burned off a large patch of this otherwise lush field. However, signs of new
vegetation are already beginning to show here and there. Looking west, a mixed
forest looms, whereas the rolling grasslands continue in the other directions.
name: Burned Field by a Forest

desc:
Frugal, however not dismal, rolling grasslands have developed in all of the
cardinal directions save west, where a tall and looming forest of fir trees
abruptly shoots straight up from the plain. A couple of bushes and boulders
provide shelter and distraction for the local wildlife. To the north, some kind
of fire seems to have swept away most of the vegetation.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
However uninhabited by the more or less intelligent races these plains may be,
they are in no way devoid of life: a fact that is prosaically demonstrated by
the innumerable piles of bird droppings that sit under this solitary, old oak.
Further east, the view is obstructed by an escarpment that runs in a north-south
direction.
name: Plains by a Solitary Tree

desc:
Rain and wind have made their mark on the rock face which blocks all passage
eastwards. In the process it has eroded the soft, reddish rock, causing plenty
of jagged shards to fall down onto this lower plain. A few bushes also grow here
close to the wall, apparently sheltered from the winds that freely roam these
forlorn fields.
name: Plains by the Escarpment

desc:
A heap of crackling twigs and branches is all that remains of the shrubbery that
grew here quite a long time ago. Now long dead, the branches serve a new purpose
in sheltering and nurturing a new generation of brush, however, still in their
infancy. Around this patch, a wild plain reaches around in all directions.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
A great slab of rock lies here almost totally embedded into the tough-looking,
bushy turf. The rock's colour is in stark contrast to the big, red boulder that
rises out of the plain south from here, telling a tale of ancient geological
upheavals. The grassy plains stretch in all directions, ultimately reaching a
reddish rock escarpment far to the east.
name: Plain by the Black Rock

desc:
Quite a few rocks and boulders have fallen down off of the rock wall east of
here, this quite recently as damage to the various bushes and briars at the foot
of the escarpment is still fully discernible. What looks like a crevice in the
rock might actually be an opening into a cave. Westwards, the rolling plains
reach out towards the tree-dotted horizon.
name: Plains by the Escarpment

desc:
Huge patches of green grass have been torn away in long patches by some kind of
torrential rains on this sloping field, and this not too long ago. East of here,
a large boulder partly obscures the view of a large and red rock escarpment that
reaches for miles and miles in a north-south alignment.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
Not quite as high as the cliff wall that is just east of here, a great, reddish
boulder stands firmly entrenched onto this juniper infested plain. The rock
serves as a lean-to for a variety of plants and shrubs. Thick, grey-green mosses
cover large parts of its surface. West, north and south from here, a rolling
plain reaches around, dotted here and there by patches of trees.
name: Plains by a Boulder

desc:
Growing next to an impressive, twenty feet tall rock wall, a few trees have
taken to clinging to the red and withered rock itself. On the ground, a few
ferns of a hardy kind, along with other plants and bushes, provide potential
cover for any number and species of animals. West, north and south of here, a
green and luscious plain reaches out, peppered here and there by dark green
clusters of trees.
name: Lower Plain

desc:
Compared to the rest of these wind-exposed plains, this area stands out as a
cosy little nook: mostly because this field is considerably lower than the ones
around it. The fact that the wind cannot exert its wear and tear on the
vegetation here, hints at a probable explanation to why the junipers grow higher
here than elsewhere.
name: Slight Depression

desc:
The monotony of these surroundings is somewhat lessened by the impressive wall
that rises out of the plains east of here. Consisting of some kind of reddish
rock, the ridge does a lot to offset the frugality of the predominantly green
plains. Far to the northwest, a shadow on the horizon gives away the location of
a dark and foreboding forest.
name: Plains

desc:
Boulders, rocks and a coarse, reddish kind of dust pepper the ground here,
obvious signs that the red-rock escarpment that lies east of here is slowly
retreating from the plain - probably due to the slow but relentless forces of
erosion: wind, rain and time. While passage east of here is hindered by the rock
wall, travel is still possible out onto the shrubby, undulating grasslands that
unfolds to the north, west and south from here.
name: Plain by an Escarpment

desc:
Quite monotonously, these forlorn plains continue in all directions, only
occasionally livened up by the odd tree or bush. Upon closer inspection, this
particular site does actually bear the marks of recent occupation by one
traveller or another: the residues of an old campfire have been wedged in
between a dog-sized boulder and a tough-looking bush, and tiny splinters of wood
lie neatly piled next to it.
name: High Plain

desc:
A brutal-looking, jagged boulder tops this location - this is the very edge of
the escarpment that effectively cuts through this landscape, dividing it into a
lower and a higher part. The boulder itself is a red, weather-worn giant, and
would provide a great look-out if it only could be climbed. Almost hidden by the
edge of the boulder, traces of a recent campfire are strewn about.
name: Sharply Rising Plain

desc:
These stark and wind-pined plains reach an abrupt end west of here, where a
sheer cliff wall sharply dives down onto a lower plain. There are a few
scattered juniper bushes growing here, and they appear to keep well away from
the rock wall. Only tough, grey-green grass interspersed with a couple of
wind-eroded boulders dares to approach the vertical rift.
name: Escarpment on the Plain

desc:
One can see quite far to the west from here, since the plain below the
escarpment is considerably lower than the wind-pined patch up here. Only the
toughest of grasses and mosses can thrive here among all of the reddish boulders
and sharp, jagged rock shards that litter the ground. Looking in all directions
save west, one's view takes in a tough and desolate plain.
name: Escarpment on the Plain

desc:
The scant vegetation that grows here is mostly comprised by tough grey-green
grasses and tough-looking waist-high juniper bushes. The austere plains are
interrupted by a sheer cliff wall that drops down onto a lower field west of
here.
name: Escarpment on the Plain

desc:
A drop in the plain west of here makes travel in that direction impossible.
However, the sloping ridge here offers easy passage to the rolling grasslands in
other directions. The great fields reach out towards what must be the mighty
river Anduin far, far to the west.
name: Escarpment on the Plain

desc:
A handful of man-sized boulders rise out of this infertile plain, contributing
to the eerie appearance of this dusty patch of land. Confusingly enough, the
boulders look nothing like the harsh reddish ground: the pinnacles are of a dead
grey hue. Upon further inspection, one can discern almost humanoid features
among the withered pillars.
name: Strange Pinnacles

desc:
The reddish earth that comprises these plains appears unable to sustain any
vegetation apart from the thick grass and low bushes that surround the site.
Thus, it is a feast for the eyes to look up from the dust and gaze westward
towards the mighty Anduin. The great river can barely be discerned far to the
west, and even further west, a jagged line that can only be the highest peaks of
the Misty Mountains rises above the horizon.
name: Reddish Plains

desc:
Among these sombre plains, one would be more than surprised to find an abundant
flora and fauna. Thus, it is not a surprise that the same tough grass and the
same tough bushes that one can find in all other cardinal directions grow here.
Looking west one can discern a sudden drop in the plain, followed by more
plains. However, a misty haze obscures the horizon: evidence of the great Anduin
that brings life to the lower plains.
name: Austere Plain

desc:
Once upon a time, this was the place where this great, old grandfather of an oak
grew in perfect solitude. However, at some point lightning struck, splitting the
trunk in two and incinerating the whole tree. The new saplings that are trying
to replace the old behemoth have their work cut out for them, trying to grow on
these barren, unforgiving plains.
name: Plains by a Burned Tree

desc:
These undulating grasslands stretch in every direction. The plain is peppered by
trees and bushes of various kinds, giving the traveller the impression of an
untouched wilderness. One may wonder if the people that once inhabited these
lands abandoned them in order to seek out the company of fellow souls, or if
they were persuaded by some other kind of force.
name: High Plain

desc:
This big cavity in the cliff face has a dusty, hard-packed dirt floor strewn
with jagged rock shards and other debris. A few giant foot-prints can be found
in the dust, allowing the world to know that the cave indeed is inhabited by
some kind of creature. From the cave's ceiling hang strands of age-old spider
webs, webs that appear to have been disturbed quite recently. Westwards, a
twelve feet high crack allows passage to the outside world. It is also possible
to venture further east into the inky darkness of the inner parts of the cave.
name: Cave Entrance

desc:
Blackish stalactites reach down from the slightly domed ceiling of this dark
cave. The floor is littered with rocks and boulders, and the rough rock walls
lean into the room, defining the shape of the room into a neat 'T'. Towards the
south, the ground rises up to meet an equally lowering ceiling, enabling access
to what appears to be a continuation of the black grotto. Just north of here, a
portal-like formation allows passage into a pitch-black section of the cave.
name: Inner Cave

desc:
The stalactite-covered ceiling here is considerably lower than the cave part
north of here. The floor is littered with something that cannot be anything but
bat excrement, a fact that is effectively confirmed by the more than musty smell
in here. North of here, the cave appears to open up a bit, but it is hard to
tell in the prevalent darkness.
name: Dead End

desc:
By any measure, this cave is a complete and utter disaster zone. Gnawed bones,
dried up pieces of hide, skulls from animals and other creatures lie thrown
about in atrocious-looking piles. One would not have to think too long and hard
to find a plausible source for the absolutely rancid smell that permeates this
square, blackened cave room. A huge, jagged rock slab has been set up against
the eastern wall, blocking further passage in that direction. South of here, the
air appears to be easier to breathe.
name: Square Lair

desc:
The most prominent feature of this narrow alcove is the stench of rotting meat
that permeates everything in here. Spoils of war and strife are littered about
on the cave floor, mostly bones and skulls that have been crushed by merciless
hands not very long ago. The only exit from this unwholesome, dark hole lies to
the west, where a big opening leads into a big, nasty-smelling cave room.
name: Collapsed Tunnel

desc:
The Anduin, which comes from the north-east in an almost straight line, suddenly
enlarges its bed, enough than to become part of the small lake that is noticed
to the west, where it accepts the waters of the River Gladden. The surrounding
riverbanks are flat, except to the south where a range of hills can be seen.
name: Near the Meeting of the Rivers

desc:
The hill which forms the eastern riverbank delimits the northern, small lake,
where the Gladden meets the Anduin, forcing the latter to shrink back to its
usual, although wide, size. After that, it appears that the river resumes its
roughly straight course towards the south-west.
name: The Anduin River

desc:
The great Anduin makes a slight bend here, before continuing with its roughly,
straight course towards the south-west. A thin, mixed forest is standing on the
eastern riverbank, the tiny waves of the river washing against some of its
nearest roots.
name: A Slight Bend in the Anduin River

desc:
While the western bank of this wide part of the Anduin is just another flat,
marshy plain, the eastern one is wooded and bordered by a thin layer of fallen
leaves. It is, however, slightly above the normal water level, and that keeps
the base of the trees safe from the river.
name: The Anduin River

desc:
Making a slow turn to the west the river heads straight for the Gladden Fields.
The landscape around is soggy, filled with pools of water and to the west a mist
is forming a few feet above the ground. From the shore long grey straws of grass
bend down in the water, touching its surface with their tips. From the muddy
bottom of the current other plants peek up in greeting.
name: Gently Flowing Anduin

desc:
The Anduin continues here on its rough path towards the southwest. While the
bottom of its wide bed is muddy and sluggish, the surrounding riverbanks are
rather flat plains. A small forest can be distinguished, beyond a couple of mild
bushes that are in growth on the eastern side.
name: The Anduin River

desc:
As the water flows gently to the south, animals can take their daily drinks here
without risk of being swept away in the currents. An occasional tree branch,
most likely ripped away by the roaring floods in the north, flows slowly down
the river. The shores are lower and less steep here, and make for a possible
crossing for anyone wanting to travel through the streaming water.
name: Calming in the Anduin

desc:
Here on the very border of the Gladden Fields the river makes a turn south
again, as if avoiding the marshy landscape. Following its path it becomes
obvious however that it cannot avoid it much longer. In this area the land is
covered in a gentle mist rising from the ground, and the fields on either side
of the current are drenched in moss and water. This was once the shores of a
great lake, but now looks more like the shores of a moss-filled sea, stretching
as far as the eye can see in the west.
name: Anduin Entering the Gladden Fields

desc:
The monotony of the mud that covers the riverbed of the Anduin is broken here
and there by large rocks, however, these rocks are not tall enough to bruise the
surface of the water. The surrounding riverbanks are quite flat, except to the
south-east, where some hills can be seen in the distance.
name: The Anduin River

desc:
The smaller, but far more clear, Gladden meets the larger Anduin in a remnant of
the former lake that once covered all of the Gladden Fields. The size of it is
now more like a large pool, though. There are quite a few eddies near the border
of the triangular mouth of the western and lesser river.
name: The Meeting of the Rivers

desc:
This cave, dug into the side of the hill, may have been an hobbit hole a long
time ago. The stone walls that surround it appear to form a rectangular shape
with rounded corners, broken to the north by a wooden door, while the earthen
ceiling is supported by large beams. There are a few, very simple pieces of
furniture, made of oak, firmly standing on the ground.
name: A Restored Hobbit Hole

desc:
A sharp drop in the plain blocks all passage westwards, as a twenty feet high
cliff wall plunges down onto the grassy plain beneath it. Along the ridge, a few
scattered bushes are kept low in stature by the winds that must blow here most
of the time. South of here one can notice the beginnings of a downward slope.
name: Escarpment on the Plain

desc:
A small field of gravel has formed into this hollow, surrounded by tall pines at
the edge of the heathlands. The source of the gravel seems to be at the hillside
in the south, where the roots of the trees and bushes growing on the slope have
failed to stop a landslide. Some dying trunks, brought down by the rolling
gravel, are lying on the bottom of the hollow while drying up and dropping their
last leaves one by one. A high hill rises above the heath in the south, but
climbing that way might be even harder than it looks.
name: A Gravel Pit

desc:
This is the highest point in this chain of hilltops stretching out to the east
and west, yet there are no good places to see far into the heathlands below.
Pine trees rise high above the ground here, and even their lowest branches seem
unreachable, while younger saplings are sprouting out of the sandy ground among
tussocks of grass. One must be careful while walking around here, for there are
rolling pine nuts and burly roots above the ground everywhere. Despite the
elevated location, even some flowering plants that normally do not thrive in
windy locations can be found on the slopes among the trees.
name: A High Hill

desc:
Rocky hills dominate the horizon, yet lower ground lies to the north and east of
here. The curvature of this landscape seems to act as a natural wind tunnel,
creating gustier conditions than one would expect. Scattered across the hills
are various types of pine trees, creating homes for small birds and animals.
Most low level foliage is scarce and sure footing is a rarity. To the west the
slope moves upward as it reaches its unimpressive apex.
name: Blustering Hills

desc:
Old and pitiful pine trees dot the area, reaching great heights but lacking many
branches. The grass here seems especially weathered, perhaps a result of high
winds coming from the south. Occasional shrubs grow between the grasses, though
it is difficult to tell if they are dead or alive.
name: Foot of the Blustering Hills

desc:
These grasslands south of an overgrown trail are rich with flowers and insects.
Small holes, not larger in diameter than the size of worm, with tiny piles of
black earth beside them, suggest recent underground activity in the fertile
soil. To the south, the bare soil appears even darker than usual, while to the
west the ground simply vanishes.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The earth rises here with a few barely noticeable hills. At a secluded part the
soil is suspiciously black and rich with compost. Its lack of crop or any other
sort of plant indicate something must have been harvested here in the recent
past. Jutting from the earth at the centre of the grasslands stands a boulder
reaching about three feet tall. In the lee of the stone to the south, a
makeshift shelter has been constructed. Some black corn cobs lie spread around a
recently burned-out camp fire.
name: Black Soil Grasslands

desc:
The ground here seems lower than the surrounding areas. Assorted grasses create
little patches of green here and there. Small clusters of purple and white wild
flowers dot the ground and provide a sweet scent to the air. To the east, a dark
patch of bare soil scars the grasslands, while to the north a deep crevice
blocks the way.
name: Dipped Grasslands

desc:
Soft, rolling fields of tall grass dominate most of this area. Very little can
be heard except the cooing of a few doves in the distance. The fragile grass
provides the wind a blank canvas, painting its every movement across the golden
fields. To the north a deep crevice blocks the way.
name: Tranquil Plains

desc:
Dominating the surroundings are what looks like the charred remains of a dense,
brush-filled flatland. An apparent brush fire must have swept through the area
recently, undoubtedly killing the plants and small animals alike. A number of
blackened stumps rise a few feet into the air with no hope of ever bearing new
growth again. Gusts of wind have blown ash through the air and most lies towards
a narrow crevice in the north.
name: Charred Brush

desc:
The uneven grassland is filled with small cracks and crevices, as if something
violent tried to reshape the landscape. In some of the moist cracks, wild
orchids can be seen within the green surroundings. To the east the grassland
ends at a rock slide, leading down into a crevice that is dozens of yards wide
and quite many feet deep.
name: Grasslands near a Crevice

desc:
The bottom of the crevice is shaded by the overhanging walls. The bedrock is
covered with a thin layer of sand coming from the sediment layers in the rock
faces, and loose rocks with sharp edges broken from the lower granite layers.
The yellow sand consists of thousands and thousands of tiny fossil skeletons of
primitive creatures. Northwards the crevice grows deeper.
name: Shaded Crevice

desc:
Various bones of large animals lie scattered between the numerous sharp rocks
that cover the solid bedrock.. The hollows between those rocks have been filled
with dirt originating from the grasslands around. Pioneer species like greater
plantain and common horsetail add some green to the otherwise grey landscape. To
the west a rock slide filling the crevice makes it possible scramble up to the
grasslands.
name: Rock Slide

desc:
This cave has been formed deep within the bedrock. The walls are covered in
chip-marks, as if this cave has been widened by simple stone-working tools.
Dripping from the ceiling, bat-guano accumulates on the floor, its stench making
breathing unpleasant. Some moss and fungi grow on the fertile compost provided
by the mixture of decomposing organic matter.
name: Dark Cave

desc:
The encompassing soil is held together by thousands of thick, gnarled roots.
Their sporadic growth patterns make any type of movement tedious. The dirt is
packed tight with the exception of the ceiling, which seems especially soft and
moist. A few sharp rocks also make this place somewhat hazardous for the unwary.
name: Underground Cave

desc:
The entire panoramic view of this enclosed area is packed full of ornamental
shrubs and flowers. Though not everything is in full bloom, it seems there is a
wide enough variety for something to be providing colour throughout all four
seasons. Around the edges of the enclosure stand tall, vine-covered walls. The
garden floor is covered with a short, thick green turf, insulating the ground
below. In the centre bubbles a fresh spring, providing life to all the
surrounding foliage.
name: An Untouched Garden

desc:
A musky smell permeates the large cave, quite likely characteristic of a large
predator. Scattered among the rocks on the floor lie the remains of several
animals. On the floor, the latest meal, a half-eaten corpse of a humanoid
creature, lies among the bones of deer and other animals. To the south the cave
continues deeper into the rocks.
name: Large Cave

desc:
An intense musky smell is pervasive in the room, giving evidence that you are
deep within the domain of whatever inhabitant this cave has. Bat-guano has
accumulated on the floor, its stench competing with the larger inhabitants'
musk. Some small ants and beetles search for food in the decomposing material on
the floor.
name: Musky Cave

desc:
Judging from the comfortable temperature, this cave must lie deep underground.
From small cracks in the ceiling mineralized water drops down forming colourful
stalactites, while the wet walls are covered with lichen and algae. A small pool
of clear water has collected in the lowest corner of the floor.
name: Hidden Cave

desc:
Among the overgrown grass and the few bushes growing here, there are a couple of
scattered slabs which are distinguished, the last remains of an old trail. This
old trail appears to be leading to the Anduin, which is flowing only a couple of
miles away to the north-west. There, it meets another old trail that lies in the
nearby south-east, at the foot of a bare hill.
name: Remains of a Trail
